---
title: "Chapter 07 — 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 06 · 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers](ch06.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [08 · 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning →](ch08.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Support Vector Machines (SVMs) are one of the most elegant ideas in classical machine learning: instead of fitting just any boundary that separates two classes, find the *single best* boundary — the one that leaves the widest possible buffer between the classes. Combined with the **kernel trick**, which lets the same linear machinery carve out curved boundaries in the original space, SVMs were the dominant classifier of the late 1990s and 2000s and remain a strong default on small, clean, high-dimensional datasets. They sit in the supervised-learning corner of classical ML, alongside the linear models of Chapter 5 and the tree ensembles of Chapter 8.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical supervised ML (discriminative classifiers) · used for binary/multiclass classification and regression on small-to-medium, high-dimensional data · the one key idea: maximize the margin, and let kernels make linear methods nonlinear.
:::

## 7.1 — The maximum-margin idea

Imagine two clouds of points on a table — red apples on the left, green ones on the right — and you must lay down a stick that separates them. If the clouds are well apart, infinitely many sticks work. Which is best? Intuition says: the stick that sits squarely in the *middle of the gap*, as far from both clouds as possible. Nudge the data a little and that stick is the least likely to suddenly misclassify a point. This is the **maximum-margin** principle, and it is the entire soul of the SVM.

Make it precise. A linear classifier in $d$ dimensions is a **hyperplane** defined by a weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ and bias $b$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b, \qquad \text{predict } +1 \text{ if } f(\mathbf{x}) > 0,\ -1 \text{ otherwise.}$$

The **margin** is the distance from the hyperplane to the nearest training point. The signed distance of a point $\mathbf{x}$ to the plane is $f(\mathbf{x}) / \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$. We are free to rescale $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ together without changing the boundary, so we fix the scale by requiring the closest points to satisfy $\lvert f(\mathbf{x})\rvert = 1$. Those points then sit at distance $1/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$ from the plane, and the full margin (both sides) is $2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$.

Maximizing $2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$ is the same as minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$, which gives the clean **hard-margin** program:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b}\ \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 \ \ \forall i.$$

Each constraint says "point $i$ is on the correct side, and at least a unit-scaled margin away." Because the objective is convex (a quadratic) and the constraints are linear, this is a **convex quadratic program** — it has a unique global optimum, with no local minima to get stuck in. That convexity, more than anything, is why SVMs were trusted: train twice, get the same answer.

## 7.2 — Support vectors, the dual, and the geometry

Here is the property that gives the method its name. When you solve that optimization, the boundary turns out to depend on only a handful of training points — the ones sitting *exactly* on the margin edges, where the constraint $y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i) = 1$ is tight. These are the **support vectors**. Every other point could be deleted and the boundary would not move at all. The SVM is, in this sense, a sparse memory of the dataset: it keeps only the borderline cases that actually define where the classes meet.

The picture below shows it. The solid line is the decision boundary; the dashed lines are the margin edges; the circled points touching the dashed lines are the support vectors that pin everything in place. Class A (squares) sits on one side, class B (triangles) on the other; the circled markers are the support vectors.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <!-- decision boundary (solid) and the two margin edges (dashed), parallel -->
  <line x1="180" y1="20" x2="180" y2="220" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="20" x2="120" y2="220" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="5,4"/>
  <line x1="240" y1="20" x2="240" y2="220" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="5,4"/>
  <!-- margin bracket between the two dashed lines -->
  <line x1="120" y1="34" x2="240" y2="34" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" marker-start="url(#a)" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="146" y="28" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">margin = 2/‖w‖</text>
  <!-- class A: squares, left -->
  <rect x="46" y="110" width="11" height="11" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.55"/>
  <rect x="70" y="60" width="11" height="11" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.55"/>
  <rect x="40" y="170" width="11" height="11" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.55"/>
  <!-- support vector on left margin edge (circled square) -->
  <rect x="114" y="124" width="12" height="12" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.55"/>
  <circle cx="120" cy="130" r="11" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- class B: triangles, right -->
  <polygon points="300,84 294,96 306,96" fill="currentColor"/>
  <polygon points="318,150 312,162 324,162" fill="currentColor"/>
  <polygon points="296,186 290,198 302,198" fill="currentColor"/>
  <!-- support vector on right margin edge (circled triangle) -->
  <polygon points="240,96 233,110 247,110" fill="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="240" cy="105" r="11" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- legend -->
  <rect x="40" y="214" width="10" height="10" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.55"/>
  <text x="55" y="223" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">class A</text>
  <polygon points="120,214 114,224 126,224" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="130" y="223" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">class B</text>
  <circle cx="232" cy="219" r="6" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="242" y="223" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">support vector</text>
</svg>

To see *why* prediction depends only on support vectors — and why kernels will work at all — you have to rewrite the problem in its **dual form**. Every constrained optimization has a twin: instead of solving for $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ directly (the *primal*), you introduce one multiplier $\alpha_i \ge 0$ per constraint and solve for those instead. For the hard-margin SVM the dual is

$$\max_{\boldsymbol\alpha}\ \sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j\, y_i y_j\, \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \alpha_i \ge 0,\ \ \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0.$$

Solve this and you recover the weights as a weighted sum over training points, $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$, where the **dual coefficients** $\alpha_i$ come out *zero for every point except the support vectors*. That is the same sparsity, now read off the math. Two facts about this dual are the whole reason the next sections work: the data appears **only** inside the inner product $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$, and prediction $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i\, \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x} + b$ does too. Replace that single dot product with something cleverer and you have the kernel trick (Section 7.4).

::: {.callout-tip}
A quick sanity check after training: if *almost every* point is a support vector, your model is likely overfitting or your kernel/regularization is mistuned. A healthy SVM keeps support vectors to a modest fraction of the data.
:::

## 7.3 — Soft margins and the C parameter

Real data is messy: classes overlap, a few points are mislabeled, and no straight line separates them cleanly. A hard margin has no answer here — the constraints are infeasible. The fix is to let some points *violate* the margin, but charge a penalty for it. We introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \ge 0$ measuring how far point $i$ pushes into or past the margin, and we pay for the total slack:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b,\boldsymbol{\xi}}\ \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.}\quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i,\ \ \xi_i \ge 0.$$

This is the **soft-margin SVM**, and the knob $C > 0$ controls the trade-off:

- **Large $C$** — violations are expensive, so the model strains to classify every point correctly. Narrow margin, low bias, high variance. Risk: overfitting noise.
- **Small $C$** — violations are cheap, so the model prefers a wide, smooth margin even if it misclassifies some training points. Wide margin, higher bias, lower variance.

So $C$ is really a **regularization** parameter in disguise: it is the inverse of how much you trust each training point. It is the single most important hyperparameter to tune (usually by cross-validation, Chapter 11), and almost always on a logarithmic grid like $\{0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$.

There is a second way to read the soft-margin objective that connects SVMs to the rest of ML. Substituting the optimal slacks turns it into an unconstrained loss:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b}\ \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2 + C\sum_i \max\!\big(0,\ 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i)\big).$$

That $\max(0, 1 - yf)$ term is the **hinge loss**: zero penalty once a point is correctly classified *with* margin, and a linearly growing penalty as it drifts inside or across the boundary. Seen this way, an SVM is just a linear model trained with hinge loss plus L2 regularization — a cousin of the logistic regression of Chapter 5, differing only in the loss function.

And because it is just hinge loss plus L2, you can train a *linear* SVM by plain gradient descent, no quadratic-program solver in sight. The subgradient of the hinge term is simple: zero when a point is comfortably correct, and $-y_i\mathbf{x}_i$ when it is inside the margin. The whole learner fits in a few lines.

```python
import numpy as np
# Linear soft-margin SVM by (sub)gradient descent on hinge loss + L2.
# Minimizes  (1/2)||w||^2 + C * sum hinge.  No QP solver needed.
def fit_linear_svm(X, y, C=1.0, lr=0.01, epochs=200):
    n, d = X.shape
    w, b = np.zeros(d), 0.0
    for _ in range(epochs):
        margins = y * (X @ w + b)            # y_i f(x_i)
        inside  = margins < 1                # points violating the margin
        # grad of (1/2)||w||^2 is w; hinge contributes -C*y_i*x_i when inside
        dw = w - C * (X[inside] * y[inside, None]).sum(axis=0)
        db =    - C *  y[inside].sum()
        w -= lr * dw
        b -= lr * db
    return w, b

# toy check: two clearly separated blobs must be classified perfectly
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
Xpos = rng.normal([ 2, 2], 0.3, (20, 2));  Xneg = rng.normal([-2,-2], 0.3, (20, 2))
X = np.vstack([Xpos, Xneg]);  y = np.r_[np.ones(20), -np.ones(20)]
w, b = fit_linear_svm(X, y)
assert (np.sign(X @ w + b) == y).all()      # separable -> 100% train accuracy
```

::: {.callout-warning}
$C$ does **not** mean "complexity" in a way that scales the same across datasets. A good $C$ depends on the feature scaling and the kernel. Always standardize your features *before* fitting an SVM (Chapter 4) — distances drive everything here, and an unscaled feature will silently dominate the margin.
:::

## 7.4 — The kernel trick

What if no straight line separates the classes — points of one class form a ring around the other? The classic move: map the data into a higher-dimensional space where it *does* become linearly separable. A 2-D ring, lifted by adding the feature $x_1^2 + x_2^2$, becomes two flat sheets in 3-D that a plane slices apart. The trouble is that explicit high-dimensional maps blow up fast — a degree-$p$ polynomial map of $d$ features has combinatorially many terms.

The **kernel trick** sidesteps this entirely. Recall from the dual (Section 7.2) that the SVM touches the data only through inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$. Suppose $\phi$ is the map into the high-dimensional space. Then we only ever need $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ — never $\phi$ itself. A **kernel** is a function that computes that inner product directly:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j).$$

If we have a cheap formula for $K$, we get all the power of the high-dimensional space at the cost of the *original* one. We never compute coordinates in the lifted space; we just compute similarities. The prediction becomes

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in \text{SV}} \alpha_i y_i\, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b.$$

For $K$ to correspond to a genuine inner product in *some* space, it must be symmetric and positive semi-definite — this is **Mercer's condition**. You rarely check it by hand; you pick from kernels already known to satisfy it.

```python
import numpy as np
# Kernel trick demo: degree-2 polynomial. The explicit feature map and
# the kernel formula must agree — that's the whole point.
def phi(x):                      # x is 2-D -> lift to 3-D
    return np.array([x[0]**2, np.sqrt(2)*x[0]*x[1], x[1]**2])

def poly_kernel(x, z):           # (x . z)^2, never builds phi
    return (x @ z) ** 2

a, b = np.array([1.0, 2.0]), np.array([3.0, -1.0])
assert np.isclose(phi(a) @ phi(b), poly_kernel(a, b))   # same number, two routes
```

The deeper payoff: kernels are not limited to vectors. Anything with a sensible similarity function — strings, graphs, sequences — can get a kernel and ride the same SVM machinery, no feature engineering required.

## 7.5 — Common kernels and how to choose

A kernel is a *similarity measure*, and the choice of kernel is the choice of what "similar" means for your problem. Three dominate practice.

| Kernel | Formula | Shape it draws | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$ | flat hyperplane | many features, few samples; text/sparse data |
| Polynomial | $(\gamma\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j + r)^p$ | curved, degree $p$ | feature interactions matter; moderate $d$ |
| RBF / Gaussian | $\exp(-\gamma\lVert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\rVert^2)$ | smooth local bumps | default for nonlinear, low-to-moderate $d$ |

In the **polynomial** row the knobs map directly to library parameters: $p$ is the **degree** (how curved the boundary may be), $r$ is an offset (sklearn's `coef0`) that trades off the influence of higher- versus lower-order terms, and $\gamma$ scales the dot product before it is raised to the power. Set $p=1, r=0$ and you are back to the linear kernel.

The **RBF (Gaussian) kernel** is the workhorse default. It measures similarity as a bump that decays with distance: nearby points are very similar, far points nearly orthogonal. Its width is set by $\gamma$ — large $\gamma$ means a narrow bump (each point influences only its immediate neighborhood, giving a wiggly, high-variance boundary), small $\gamma$ means a broad bump (smooth, almost linear). The RBF map is formally *infinite-dimensional*, which is why it can represent such flexible boundaries — and exactly why $\gamma$ (together with $C$) is what reins that flexibility back in and keeps it from memorizing the training set.

You do not have to guess $\gamma$ from nothing: its sane default ties to the data's scale. Sklearn's `gamma='scale'` sets $\gamma = 1/(d\cdot\mathrm{Var}(X))$ — one over the feature count times the feature variance — so the kernel starts out calibrated to how spread-out your (standardized) features are. Tuning then means searching *around* that anchor, not across blind orders of magnitude.

Tuning an RBF SVM is a **two-dimensional grid search** over $(C, \gamma)$, evaluated by cross-validation. The two interact: both push toward more flexible fits, so you sweep them jointly, not one at a time.

::: {.callout-tip}
Start with the **linear kernel** if your feature count is large relative to your sample count (e.g. bag-of-words text). High-dimensional data is often already linearly separable, and a linear SVM trains far faster and rarely overfits. Reach for RBF only when a linear model underfits.
:::

## 7.6 — Multiclass SVMs

An SVM is, at its core, a *binary* machine: one hyperplane splits the world into two sides. So how does it handle handwritten digits with ten classes? You don't — you stitch together many binary SVMs and let them vote. Two schemes dominate:

- **One-vs-rest (OvR)** — train $k$ classifiers for $k$ classes, each one separating "class $i$" from "all the others." At prediction time, run all $k$ and pick the class whose decision function scores highest. Cheap ($k$ models) but the sub-problems are imbalanced (one class versus the rest).
- **One-vs-one (OvO)** — train one classifier for every *pair* of classes, $\binom{k}{2} = k(k-1)/2$ of them, each seeing only its two classes' data. Predict by majority vote across all pairwise duels. More models, but each trains on less data, which keeps it tractable.

The standard SVM library, **libsvm** (the engine under sklearn's `SVC`), uses **one-vs-one**. It sounds wasteful at $O(k^2)$ classifiers, but because each is trained on only the two relevant classes rather than the whole dataset, the total cost stays manageable, and it sidesteps the class-imbalance that dogs OvR.

## 7.7 — Support Vector Regression (SVR)

The same margin idea flips neatly to regression. In classification the margin is a *gap to keep empty*; in **Support Vector Regression** it becomes a *tube to keep points inside*. We fit a function and declare any prediction within $\pm\epsilon$ of the true value "good enough" — zero loss. Only points falling *outside* the tube are penalized.

This is the **$\epsilon$-insensitive loss**:

$$L_\epsilon(y, f(\mathbf{x})) = \max\big(0,\ \lvert y - f(\mathbf{x})\rvert - \epsilon\big).$$

The effect is a flat-bottomed loss: small errors cost nothing, which makes SVR robust to small noise and, like classification SVMs, sparse — only the points on or outside the tube (the support vectors) determine the fit. The $\epsilon$ parameter sets the tube width (how much error you ignore), while $C$ again trades off flatness of the function against tolerance for points outside the tube. And because SVR is kernelized too, swapping in an RBF kernel gives smooth nonlinear regression with the same code path.

## 7.8 — Strengths, limits, and when to reach for an SVM

SVMs occupy a specific sweet spot, and knowing it saves you from forcing them where they don't belong.

**Strengths.** Effective in high-dimensional spaces, even when features outnumber samples — the margin principle is a built-in regularizer. The optimization is convex, so the solution is unique and reproducible. Kernels make them flexible without explicit feature engineering. The model is sparse, depending only on support vectors.

**Limits.** Training scales poorly — roughly between $O(n^2)$ and $O(n^3)$ in the number of samples — so SVMs are impractical past tens of thousands of points (use a linear SVM with stochastic solvers, or move on). They give no natural probabilities: the decision function outputs a signed distance, not a probability, so you must bolt on **Platt scaling**, which fits a logistic sigmoid $\sigma(a f(\mathbf{x}) + b)$ to the scores on held-out data to turn them into calibrated probabilities. They are sensitive to feature scaling and to $(C, \gamma)$ tuning. And the resulting model, especially with RBF, is hard to interpret.

How they compare to the obvious alternatives:

| Method | Shines when | Struggles with |
|---|---|---|
| **SVM (RBF)** | small/medium, high-dim, clean data; need max accuracy on a hard boundary | large $n$, raw interpretability, native probabilities |
| **Tree ensembles** (Ch. 8) | tabular data with mixed types, missing values, large $n$; want feature importance | very high-dim sparse data; smooth boundaries |
| **Neural nets** (Ch. 14+) | huge data, images/text/audio, learned representations | small data (overfit), tuning cost, compute |

The practical rule: on a few thousand clean, numeric, high-dimensional rows where accuracy matters more than interpretability, an RBF SVM is hard to beat and quick to try. On large messy tabular data, reach for gradient-boosted trees. On raw perceptual data at scale, reach for deep nets.

## 7.x — Key takeaways

- An SVM finds the **maximum-margin** separating hyperplane — the boundary with the widest buffer to the nearest points, giving good generalization.
- Only the **support vectors** (points on the margin edge) define the boundary; the rest are irrelevant, making the model sparse. The **dual** form makes this explicit and exposes the inner products kernels exploit.
- **Hard margin** requires perfect separation; **soft margin** allows violations penalized by $C$. Large $C$ = fit hard (overfit risk), small $C$ = wide smooth margin (regularize). Equivalent to hinge loss + L2, trainable by plain gradient descent.
- The **kernel trick** computes inner products in a high-dimensional space directly via $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)$, giving nonlinear boundaries at linear cost. RBF is the default ($\gamma$ controls bump width, `scale` is its sane anchor); linear for high-dim sparse data.
- SVMs are binary; **multiclass** comes from stitching binary models together — one-vs-rest or, as libsvm does, **one-vs-one**.
- **SVR** swaps the margin for an $\epsilon$-tube: errors inside the tube are free, points outside are support vectors.
- Always **scale features first**; tune $(C, \gamma)$ by cross-validation. Best for small-to-medium, high-dimensional, clean datasets; poor scaling rules out very large $n$. Probabilities require **Platt scaling**.

## 7.y — See also

- **Chapter 05 — Linear & Logistic Models**: the linear classifier SVMs build on; hinge loss vs. logistic loss.
- **Chapter 08 — Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning**: the go-to alternative for large, mixed-type tabular data.
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering**: feature scaling, without which SVMs misbehave.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection**: cross-validation for tuning $C$ and $\gamma$, and probability calibration (Platt scaling).
- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning**: convex optimization, inner products, and the duality behind the kernel trick.
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning**: kernel methods reappear in kernel PCA.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 06 · 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers](ch06.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [08 · 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning →](ch08.html)
